# Pushing the Jira stories with Haiku 4.5

Copy-paste prompts for **Claude Haiku 4.5** to create the Jira issues from the generated CSVs. Jira only —
Confluence is a separate job.

**The artifact already carries everything** (regenerated by `generate_jira.py`), so the prompts stay simple
and tool-independent:

- **One shared epic — already created.** `Federate BreakDown Product` exists in Jira; every Story/Spike row's
  `Epic Link` column points at it by name. The prompts **do not create an epic** — they link to yours. Have
  its key ready as `<EPIC_KEY>`.
- **Domain-tagged summaries** — each `Summary` is already `[<Domain>] <Story ID> · <title>`
  (e.g. `[BOM] BOM-BE-E-01 · updateBom — 3-step orchestrated write`). The story ID comes straight from the
  artifact. Nothing to prefix at push time.
- **Source:** `output/jira/all-stories.csv` = 1 Epic row + **337 Story rows + 6 program-Spike rows** (343
  total) across all 13 domains. Per-domain files (`bom.csv`, `product.csv`, …) carry the same shared epic and
  hold **only that domain's Story rows** (no spikes) if you'd rather push one domain.
- **The 6 program spikes** (`SPIKE-01…06`) are cross-domain — they live **only in `all-stories.csv`**
  (Issue Type=Spike), not in any per-domain file. If you push per-domain, push the spikes once too (see below).
- **Complex cross-domain cases import separately** — each `output/complexStories/<case>/<case>.csv` is
  imported under its home-stub story, not via `all-stories.csv`.

> **Tooling is unknown / not Atlassian-specific.** Your org's push tool might be the Atlassian MCP, or a
> custom tool. **These prompts don't name any tool** — the agent uses whatever Jira create/search/link
> capability it has. Step 0 makes it discover that first.

---

## Do we do one prompt for all domains?

**Yes.** Point it at `output/jira/all-stories.csv` and it creates the one epic + all 343 issues + all
dependency links in a single autonomous run. That's the **open instruction** below.

Caveat: over ~340 issues in one conversation Haiku can drift (skip a row, mis-map a dependency). The open
prompt defends against this by telling it to **work in batches, write a durable ID→key map file, and label
each issue with our Story ID** — so nothing depends on it remembering 343 keys. If you'd rather review
between phases, use the **step-by-step** version instead (same work, split into prompts).

---

## Option A — One open instruction, per domain (run once per domain)

Recommended: run the same autonomous prompt against **one domain file at a time**. Smaller context per run
(each domain is ≤71 rows), cheaper, and if a domain fails you re-drive just that one. Every domain links to
the **same existing epic** and appends to the **same** `jira-key-map.csv`, so the domains add up cleanly.

Paste this once per domain. Replace `<PROJECT_KEY>`, `<EPIC_KEY>` (existing "Federate BreakDown Product"),
and `<DOMAIN>` (e.g. `bom`, `product` — the file name without `.csv`).

```
You are pushing ONE domain of our migration backlog into Jira project <PROJECT_KEY>, under the EXISTING epic
<EPIC_KEY> ("Federate BreakDown Product"). Source file: output/jira/<DOMAIN>.csv. Work autonomously through
this whole file; only stop if a tool is missing or you hit an error you can't resolve.

SETUP
- First, list the Jira tools you actually have (create issue, search issues, link issues) and confirm the
  project has an issue-link type meaning "Blocks / is blocked by". If a "Spike" issue type doesn't exist,
  create Spike rows as Story with an extra label "spike". Tell me what you found, then continue.
- The epic already exists — do NOT create an epic. Skip the row with Issue Type=Epic entirely.

CSV columns: Issue Type, Story ID, Summary, Epic Name, Epic Link, Phase, T-shirt size, Labels, Labels,
Labels, Parent Link, Depends On, Status, Description.

1. STORIES/SPIKES: for every row with Issue Type=Story or Spike (process in batches of ~20 so you stay
   accurate):
   - issue type per the row (Spike -> Spike type, else Story-with-"spike"-label).
   - summary = the Summary column VERBATIM (it already begins with "[<Domain>] <StoryID> ·" — do not change it).
   - description = the Description column with its FORMATTING PRESERVED: it is multi-paragraph
     (blank-line separated sections) with light markup (*emphasis*, **bold**, `code`, "- " bullets,
     [ ] checklists). Keep every paragraph break and convert the markup to this Jira's description
     format (wiki markup or rich text). Never collapse to one line, strip markup, or reword.
   - labels = the three Labels values, plus "phase-<Phase>", "size-<T-shirt size>", and "id-<Story ID>"
     (e.g. id-BOM-BE-S-01). The id- label is required.
   - link the issue to epic <EPIC_KEY>.
   - IDEMPOTENT: before creating, search for label "id-<Story ID>"; if it exists, skip (don't duplicate).
   - After each batch, append "<Story ID>,<Jira Key>" lines to output/jira/jira-key-map.csv
     (header "Story ID,Jira Key" if new). This file accumulates across all domains — never overwrite it.

2. DEPENDENCIES (after this domain's issues exist): for every row with a non-empty "Depends On", read the
   dependent's key and each dependency's key from output/jira/jira-key-map.csv, then create a "Blocks" link
   (dependency blocks the dependent). Depends On sometimes uses short ids like "B-01" — match by suffix within
   this domain (SPARK-<CODE>-B-01). If an id isn't in the map yet (it may belong to a domain not pushed yet),
   record it as unresolved; never guess a key.  Skip links that already exist.

3. VERIFY & REPORT: count issues you created/skipped for this domain vs the Story/Spike rows in
   output/jira/<DOMAIN>.csv; list any row with no issue and any Depends On entry with no link (note which
   unresolved ids point at another domain). Give me the counts and the gap list.
```

**Run order — repeat for each domain** (any order; counts = Story + Spike rows):

| `<DOMAIN>` | rows | | `<DOMAIN>` | rows |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `attachment` | 24 | | `impression` | 7 |
| `bom` | 36 | | `product` | 67 |
| `claims` | 20 | | `productDetails` | 13 |
| `discussion` | 32 | | `sample` | 28 |
| `measurement` | 20 | | `search` | 25 |
| `packaging` | 24 | | `watchlist` | 13 |
| `workspace` | 28 | | | |

> **Rows = Story rows only** (337 total). The **6 program spikes** aren't here — after the domains, push them
> once: run the Option-A prompt against `output/jira/all-stories.csv` with an idempotent skip (it re-creates
> nothing that already has an `id-…` label, so only the 6 `SPIKE-0x` rows land).

**After the last domain — one cross-domain sweep** (catches any dependency that pointed at a domain pushed
later, which was "unresolved" at the time):
```
All 13 domains are pushed. Do a final dependency pass over output/jira/all-stories.csv using
output/jira/jira-key-map.csv: for every "Depends On" that has no Jira link yet, create the "Blocks" link now
(both keys should be in the map). Report links added and anything still unresolved.
```

> Want it all in one shot instead? Same prompt, but set the source to `output/jira/all-stories.csv` (one run,
> all 343 Story/Spike rows). Riskier for Haiku over 340+ issues — the per-domain runs above are steadier.

---

## Option B — Step by step (same work, review between phases)

Use these if you want to check each phase before the next. Per-domain: swap `all-stories.csv` for a single
domain file (e.g. `output/jira/bom.csv`) but keep linking to the **same** epic.

**Step 0 — discover tools**
```
List the Jira tools you have (create issue, search, link issues). Confirm project <PROJECT_KEY> has a
"Blocks / is blocked by" link type, and whether it has a "Spike" issue type (if not we'll use Story + label
"spike"). Report and STOP.
```

**Step 1 — confirm the existing epic**
```
The epic "Federate BreakDown Product" already exists as <EPIC_KEY>. Find it and confirm the key resolves in
<PROJECT_KEY>. Do NOT create an epic. Report what you found and STOP.
```

**Step 2 — create stories/spikes (repeat per batch)**
```
Create Jira issues for rows 2–21 of output/jira/all-stories.csv (skip Epic rows), in <PROJECT_KEY>, linked
to epic <EPIC_KEY>.
- summary = the Summary column verbatim (already "[<Domain>] <StoryID> · ...").
- description = Description with formatting preserved (multi-paragraph + *emphasis*/`code`/bullets —
  convert to this Jira's format; never flatten or strip).
- labels = the three Labels + "phase-<Phase>" + "size-<T-shirt size>" + "id-<Story ID>".
- Spike rows -> Spike type (or Story + label "spike").
- Before each: search label "id-<Story ID>"; skip if it exists.
- Append "<Story ID>,<Jira Key>" per issue to output/jira/jira-key-map.csv.
- Ignore Depends On for now.
Report: Story ID | domain | key | created/skipped. STOP.
```
Repeat with rows 22–41, 42–61, … until done, then:
```
Confirm output/jira/jira-key-map.csv has 343 data lines and list any Story IDs from all-stories.csv missing
from it.
```

**Step 3 — link dependencies**
```
Using only output/jira/all-stories.csv (Depends On) and output/jira/jira-key-map.csv (Story ID -> key):
for each row with a non-empty Depends On, create a "Blocks" link where each dependency blocks the row.
Match short ids like "B-01" by suffix within the same domain. Unresolvable ids -> report, don't guess.
Skip links that already exist. Report links created and any unresolved ids. STOP.
```

**Step 4 — verify**
```
Count issues linked to epic <EPIC_KEY> vs the 343 Story/Spike rows. List any CSV row with no issue and any
Depends On with no link. Report gaps only.
```

---

## Re-sync after regenerating the CSVs

Safe and non-duplicating because every issue has an `id-<Story ID>` label and the map file exists:

```
The CSVs were regenerated. Re-sync output/jira/all-stories.csv into <PROJECT_KEY> without duplicates:
for each Story/Spike row, search "id-<Story ID>" — if it exists, update its summary/description/labels in
place; if not, create it (as above) and append to jira-key-map.csv. Skip Epic rows. Then add only missing
dependency links. Report created/updated/unchanged counts.
```

---

## Notes

- **Formatting:** since `generate_jira.py` emits multi-paragraph descriptions, any re-push must keep the
  paragraph structure — a re-sync that flattens descriptions is a regression, not a no-op. The re-sync
  prompt's "update in place" includes restoring formatting if an earlier push flattened it.
- **Why two passes (create, then link):** Depends On references our Story IDs, which have no Jira key until
  the issue exists. The `jira-key-map.csv` file is the bridge — keep it; it's also what later wires Confluence
  pages to live Jira status.
- **Per-domain run:** BOM = 36 stories, Product = 67 — **no spikes in per-domain files** (the 6 program spikes
  are in `all-stories.csv` only). Smaller context per run, cheaper, easier to re-drive if one domain fails —
  but every domain still links to the one `Federate BreakDown Product` epic.
- **If your tool is a native CSV importer** (not an agent): import `all-stories.csv` — domain-tagged
  summaries, labels, and the `Epic Link` (by name) are all in the file, so rows attach to the existing
  `Federate BreakDown Product` epic. **Delete the `Issue Type=Epic` row first** (the epic already exists —
  importing it risks a duplicate). Only the Depends On links need the agent afterwards (importers can't
  resolve our-ID → Jira-key).
